perturbation.original_text: Any disputes arising herein shall be settled in a court in FLorida.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising herein shall be settled via mandatory arbitration, with the arbitrator selected at Galacticomm's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution from a court in Florida to mandatory arbitration where one party selects the arbitrator can be problematic. While arbitration is generally enforceable, complete control over arbitrator selection by one party can raise concerns about fairness and impartiality, potentially conflicting with established principles of contract law and due process.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA allows for vacating an arbitration award if there is evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrator. Granting one party sole discretion in selecting the arbitrator creates an appearance of partiality, making the agreement potentially unenforceable under the FAA.

Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration- (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon s
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces mandatory arbitration with the arbitrator selected at Galacticomm's sole discretion, replacing the original clause specifying resolution in a Florida court. The Law Explanation states that granting one party sole discretion in selecting the arbitrator creates an appearance of partiality, potentially making the agreement unenforceable under the FAA. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 9 USC 10(a)(2), allows a court to vacate an arbitration award where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators. By allowing Galacticomm to solely select the arbitrator, the changed text creates a situation where evident partiality could be argued, directly contradicting the principles outlined in 9 USC 10(a)(2), thus creating a legal contradiction as the contract language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Galacticomm must provide a person(s) to correct any technical problems (Server being down or inaccessible) 24 hours per day, 7 days per week. This person(s) must be available by beeper or telephone.
perturbation.changed_text: Galacticomm will endeavor to provide assistance with technical problems as resources allow. Assistance will be provided during normal business hours, excluding holidays.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies a 24/7 support obligation. The modified version weakens this to 'endeavor to provide assistance as resources allow' and limits it to 'normal business hours,' directly contradicting the initial commitment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract Principles - Promissory Estoppel
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the support obligation, Galacticomm could face a claim of breach of contract. Further, if Horst relied on the 24/7 support promise to their detriment (e.g., by not hiring their own support staff), promissory estoppel could apply, making the original promise enforceable despite the modification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: promissory estoppel | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you promissory estoppel Overview Within contract law , promissory estoppel refers to the doctrine that a party may recover on the basis of a promise made when the party's reliance on that promise was reasonable , and the party attempting to recover detrimentally relied on the promise. Recognition In Cohen v. Cowles Media Co . 501 US 663 (1991) , the Supreme Court recognized promissory estoppel as a "state law doctrine creating legal obligations never explicitly assumed by the parties that are enforceable." Consequences An agreement made by promissory estoppel will typically have the same binding effects on parties that a valid contract would. If a party breaches an obligation created by promissory estoppel, a court can choose to assign either reliance damages or expectation damages . Further Reading For more on promissory estoppel, see this Fordham Law Review article , this UCLA Law Review article , and this University of Chicago Law Review article . Keywords contract contract law promissory estoppel ESTOPPEL BILATERAL CONTRACTS Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 24/7 support, while the modified version weakens this obligation to assistance 'as resources allow' during 'normal business hours.' This contradicts the initial commitment. The Law Explanation mentions 'breach of contract' and 'promissory estoppel' if the original promise is relied upon. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines promissory estoppel as applying when 'a party's reliance on that promise was reasonable, and the party attempting to recover detrimentally relied on the promise.' Thus, the changed text introduces a legal contradiction by significantly reducing the level of support initially guaranteed and potentially creating grounds for a claim of promissory estoppel.

perturbation.original_text: Horst Entertainment agrees to pay Galactcomm $0.01 (one cent) per access up to 400,000 accesses thereafter payment shall be $0.005 (one-half cent) per access.
perturbation.changed_text: Horst Entertainment agrees to pay Galacticomm a mutually agreed-upon fee per access, subject to change at Galacticomm's discretion based on market conditions.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies a clear pricing structure. The modified version makes the price per access 'mutually agreed-upon' but 'subject to change at Galacticomm's discretion'. This creates uncertainty and allows Galacticomm to unilaterally alter the payment terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-305. Open Price Term
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-305 addresses open price terms. While it allows for prices to be set later, it requires good faith and commercial reasonableness. Giving one party the sole discretion to change the price without objective standards violates the good faith requirement, potentially rendering the price term unenforceable. Note that this is particularly pertinent if the software is considered a "good" rather than a "service" under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract allows Galacticomm to unilaterally change the price, which directly contradicts the UCC § 2-305 requirement for good faith in setting prices. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (2), explicitly states that 'A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith.' The original contract provides a clear pricing structure, while the modified version gives Galacticomm sole discretion, potentially violating the good faith requirement.
